meta.notes: Lab equipment database for IB Chemistry simulations. Contains equipment specs, IB-specific guidance, confusion groups for trainer exercises, scenario-based selection problems, and precision calculation data.

equipment.name: Burette
equipment.typicalUse: Accurate measurement of variable volumes in titrations
equipment.ibNotes: Read bottom of meniscus. Record to 2 d.p. (e.g., 23.45 cm³). Two readings needed per titre so total uncertainty is ±0.10 cm³.
commonMistakes: Forgetting to rinse with titrant before filling
Forgetting that total uncertainty = 2 × reading uncertainty

equipment.name: Volumetric Pipette
equipment.typicalUse: Delivers a fixed, precise volume (e.g., 25.00 cm³)
equipment.ibNotes: Touch tip to flask wall to deliver last drop. Do NOT blow out residual liquid.
commonMistakes: Using wrong size for required volume

equipment.name: Graduated Pipette
equipment.typicalUse: Measures variable small volumes with moderate precision
equipment.ibNotes: Two readings required (initial and final) so total uncertainty is ±0.2 cm³. Use for volumes where a volumetric pipette is not available.
commonMistakes: Forgetting that two readings double the uncertainty
Using when a volumetric pipette would be more precise

equipment.name: Measuring Cylinder
equipment.typicalUse: Measures approximate volumes of liquids
equipment.ibNotes: Choose the smallest cylinder that fits the required volume to minimise percentage uncertainty. Read bottom of meniscus at eye level.
commonMistakes: Using a large cylinder for a small volume (high % uncertainty)
Not reading at eye level (parallax error)

equipment.name: Volumetric Flask
equipment.typicalUse: Prepares solutions of precise concentration
equipment.ibNotes: Fill to calibration line with bottom of meniscus touching the mark. Invert several times to mix thoroughly.
commonMistakes: Not mixing thoroughly after making up to the mark
Dissolving solute directly in the flask instead of a beaker first

equipment.name: Beaker
equipment.typicalUse: General purpose container for mixing and heating
equipment.ibNotes: Graduations are approximate only. Never use a beaker for accurate volume measurement.
commonMistakes: Using beaker graduations for accurate measurement

equipment.name: Conical (Erlenmeyer) Flask
equipment.typicalUse: Titration receiving flask; swirling without spilling
equipment.ibNotes: Used to receive titrant during titration. The conical shape allows swirling without splashing. Volume markings are not used for measurement.
commonMistakes: Using the volume markings for measurement

equipment.name: Round-Bottom Flask
equipment.typicalUse: Even heating; used in distillation and reflux
equipment.ibNotes: Round shape distributes heat evenly and resists thermal stress. Must be supported with a clamp on a retort stand.

equipment.name: Test Tube
equipment.typicalUse: Small-scale reactions and qualitative tests
equipment.ibNotes: Point away from people when heating. Use a test tube holder, not fingers. Fill to about one-third for reactions.

equipment.name: Boiling Tube
equipment.typicalUse: Heating larger volumes; vigorous reactions
equipment.ibNotes: Wider than a standard test tube. Better for vigorous reactions or larger volumes that need heating.

equipment.name: Watch Glass
equipment.typicalUse: Covering beakers; holding small solid samples
equipment.ibNotes: Place over beaker to reduce evaporation or prevent splashing. Can also be used to weigh small solid samples.
commonMistakes: Using to weigh volatile or hygroscopic substances without cover

equipment.name: Evaporating Dish
equipment.typicalUse: Evaporating solutions to obtain solid crystals
equipment.ibNotes: Heat gently to avoid spitting. For crystallisation, stop heating before all liquid has evaporated.
commonMistakes: Evaporating to complete dryness when crystals are needed

equipment.name: Bunsen Burner
equipment.typicalUse: Strong direct heating with adjustable flame
equipment.ibNotes: Blue flame (air hole open) for strong heating. Yellow/safety flame (air hole closed) when not actively heating. Never use near flammable solvents.
commonMistakes: Leaving air hole open when not heating (invisible blue flame)

equipment.name: Hot Plate
equipment.typicalUse: Gentle, controlled heating without open flame
equipment.ibNotes: Preferred over Bunsen burner when using flammable solvents. Temperature can be set and maintained.
commonMistakes: Forgetting surface stays hot after switching off
Using for reactions requiring very high temperatures

equipment.name: Electronic Balance
equipment.typicalUse: Accurate mass measurement
equipment.ibNotes: Tare (zero) before each measurement. Record to the precision shown on display.
commonMistakes: Not closing draught doors on analytical balance
Recording fewer decimal places than shown

equipment.name: Thermometer
equipment.typicalUse: Measuring temperature of solutions
equipment.ibNotes: Wait for reading to stabilise. Immerse bulb fully in liquid.

equipment.name: Filter Funnel
equipment.typicalUse: Gravity filtration to separate insoluble solids
equipment.ibNotes: Use with fluted filter paper for faster flow. Pour down a glass rod to avoid splashing.
commonMistakes: Not using a glass rod to guide the liquid

equipment.name: Separating Funnel
equipment.typicalUse: Separating immiscible liquids by density
equipment.ibNotes: Open stopcock at top to allow air in when draining. Drain lower layer first through the tap.
commonMistakes: Forgetting to open the stopper when draining
Not inverting and venting during extraction
Draining too much of the lower layer

equipment.name: Crucible
equipment.typicalUse: Heating solids to very high temperatures
equipment.ibNotes: Use with a pipe-clay triangle on a tripod. Heat to constant mass for gravimetric analysis.

equipment.name: Mortar and Pestle
equipment.typicalUse: Grinding solids into fine powder
equipment.ibNotes: Grinding increases surface area, which increases reaction rate. Use gentle circular motions.

equipment.name: Wash Bottle
equipment.typicalUse: Rinsing apparatus with distilled water
equipment.ibNotes: Use distilled water to rinse walls of conical flask during titration. Adding rinse water does not affect titre result.
commonMistakes: Using tap water instead of distilled water
Using too much water (dilutes solution unnecessarily)

equipment.name: Dropping Pipette
equipment.typicalUse: Adding small amounts of liquid drop by drop
equipment.ibNotes: Used for adding indicator or small reagent quantities. Not suitable for accurate volume measurement.
commonMistakes: Cross-contaminating by dipping into different solutions

equipment.name: Stirring Rod
equipment.typicalUse: Stirring solutions and aiding dissolution
equipment.ibNotes: Also used to guide liquid when pouring into a filter funnel or narrow vessel.

equipment.name: Tripod and Wire Gauze
equipment.typicalUse: Supporting containers above a Bunsen burner
equipment.ibNotes: Wire gauze with ceramic centre spreads heat evenly. Place beaker or evaporating dish on top.
commonMistakes: Not centring the gauze over the flame
Placing heavy apparatus without checking stability

equipment.name: Retort Stand and Clamp
equipment.typicalUse: Holding and supporting apparatus at height
equipment.ibNotes: Use boss head and clamp to secure burettes, round-bottom flasks, and condensers at the required height.
commonMistakes: Placing base too far from apparatus (toppling risk)

equipment.name: Safety Goggles
equipment.typicalUse: Protecting eyes from chemical splashes
equipment.ibNotes: Must be worn at all times during practical work. Required whenever chemicals, glassware, or heat sources are in use.
commonMistakes: Wearing on forehead instead of over eyes

equipment.name: Pipette Filler
equipment.typicalUse: Safely drawing liquid into pipettes
equipment.ibNotes: Never pipette by mouth. Use filler bulb or wheel device.

equipment.name: Analytical Balance
equipment.typicalUse: Precise mass measurement to 3 decimal places
equipment.ibNotes: Close draught shield before reading. Tare before each measurement.

equipment.name: pH Meter
equipment.typicalUse: Accurate pH measurement of solutions
equipment.ibNotes: Calibrate with buffer solutions before use. Rinse probe between samples.

equipment.name: Digital Thermometer
equipment.typicalUse: Precise digital temperature reading
equipment.ibNotes: More precise than glass thermometer. Often used with data loggers.
commonMistakes: Not waiting for reading to stabilise
Not immersing probe tip fully in liquid

equipment.name: Stopwatch
equipment.typicalUse: Timing reactions and processes
equipment.ibNotes: Human reaction time (~0.3 s) usually dominates over instrument uncertainty.

equipment.name: Test Tube Rack
equipment.typicalUse: Holding test tubes upright during reactions
equipment.ibNotes: Keeps test tubes organised and upright. Label tubes to avoid mix-ups.
commonMistakes: Not labelling test tubes in the rack

equipment.name: Buchner Funnel
equipment.typicalUse: Vacuum filtration for faster solid separation
equipment.ibNotes: Used with side-arm flask and vacuum pump. Faster than gravity filtration.

equipment.name: Liebig Condenser
equipment.typicalUse: Cooling vapours during distillation
equipment.ibNotes: Water flows in at bottom, out at top (countercurrent). Used in distillation.

equipment.name: Reflux Condenser
equipment.typicalUse: Condensing vapours back into reaction flask
equipment.ibNotes: Vertical setup. Prevents loss of volatile reactants during heating.
commonMistakes: Sealing the top (must remain open to prevent pressure buildup)

equipment.name: Distillation Adapter
equipment.typicalUse: Connecting flask to condenser in distillation setup
equipment.ibNotes: Angled adapter directs vapours from flask down into the condenser.
commonMistakes: Incorrect angle causing condensate to flow back

equipment.name: Side-Arm Flask
equipment.typicalUse: Connects to vacuum for Buchner filtration
equipment.ibNotes: Heavy-walled to withstand reduced pressure. Also called filter flask.
commonMistakes: Using a regular flask under vacuum (implosion risk)

equipment.name: Water Bath
equipment.typicalUse: Gentle, even heating at controlled temperature
equipment.ibNotes: Preferred for flammable solvents. Max ~100°C at atmospheric pressure.
commonMistakes: Using with Bunsen when flammable solvents present

equipment.name: Heat-Resistant Mat
equipment.typicalUse: Protecting bench from hot apparatus
equipment.ibNotes: Place under hot beakers, tripods, and crucibles to prevent bench damage.
commonMistakes: Placing hot glassware directly on bench

equipment.name: Magnetic Stirrer
equipment.typicalUse: Continuous stirring of solutions
equipment.ibNotes: Drop stirrer bar into flask, place on magnetic stirrer plate.
commonMistakes: Setting speed too high (splashing or vortex)
Forgetting to remove stirrer bar before pouring

equipment.name: Lab Coat
equipment.typicalUse: Protecting skin and clothing from chemical splashes
equipment.ibNotes: Must be worn buttoned up during all practical work. Cotton preferred over synthetic.

equipment.name: Nitrile Gloves
equipment.typicalUse: Protecting hands from corrosive or toxic chemicals
equipment.ibNotes: Use when handling concentrated acids/bases, organic solvents, or toxic substances.
commonMistakes: Not checking for tears before use
Touching face or phone while wearing gloves

equipment.name: Fume Hood
equipment.typicalUse: Ventilated workspace for reactions producing toxic fumes
equipment.ibNotes: Keep sash low. Use for HCl gas, bromine, concentrated ammonia, etc.
commonMistakes: Raising sash too high during reaction

equipment.name: Waste Containers
equipment.typicalUse: Proper disposal of chemical waste by category
equipment.ibNotes: Separate aqueous, organic, and solid waste. Never pour organic solvents down sink.
commonMistakes: Pouring organic solvents down the sink

equipment.name: Filter Paper
equipment.typicalUse: Trapping insoluble solids during filtration
equipment.ibNotes: Fold into cone for gravity filtration, flat circle for Buchner.
commonMistakes: Using wrong grade for the particle size

equipment.name: Chromatography Paper
equipment.typicalUse: Separating mixtures by differential migration
equipment.ibNotes: Spot must be above solvent level. Pencil line (not pen) for origin.
commonMistakes: Using pen instead of pencil for origin

equipment.name: Desiccator
equipment.typicalUse: Storing samples in dry atmosphere
equipment.ibNotes: Contains desiccant (silica gel). Used to cool samples to constant mass.

equipment.name: pH Sensor
equipment.typicalUse: Digital pH probe for data logging
equipment.ibNotes: Vernier/Pasco sensor for continuous pH monitoring. Calibrate with buffers.

equipment.name: Conductivity Probe
equipment.typicalUse: Measuring solution conductivity
equipment.ibNotes: Useful for monitoring ionic concentration changes in reactions.

equipment.name: Drying Oven
equipment.typicalUse: Drying samples to constant mass
equipment.ibNotes: Typically set to 100-110°C. Use with desiccator for cooling.
commonMistakes: Not cooling in desiccator before weighing
Setting temperature too high for heat-sensitive samples

scenarios.task: Measure exactly 25.00 cm³ of a standard solution for a titration.
scenarios.explanation: A volumetric pipette delivers a fixed, precise volume with low uncertainty (±0.04 cm³).

scenarios.task: Accurately measure varying volumes of acid added during a titration.
scenarios.explanation: A burette allows precise measurement of variable volumes (±0.05 cm³ per reading).

scenarios.task: Prepare exactly 250 cm³ of a standard solution from a weighed solid.
scenarios.explanation: A volumetric flask is calibrated to contain an exact volume for solution preparation.

scenarios.task: Separate two immiscible liquids (e.g., oil and water).
scenarios.explanation: A separating funnel allows layers to be drained separately via the stopcock.

scenarios.task: Remove an insoluble solid from a liquid by filtration.
scenarios.explanation: Gravity filtration uses a filter funnel with paper to trap insoluble solids.

scenarios.task: Heat a solution strongly and quickly over a direct flame.
scenarios.explanation: A Bunsen burner provides the highest temperature with an adjustable flame.

scenarios.task: Evaporate a solution slowly to obtain salt crystals.
scenarios.explanation: An evaporating dish has a large surface area for controlled evaporation.

scenarios.task: Weigh a sample of solid accurately to ±0.01 g.
scenarios.explanation: An electronic balance provides accurate mass readings with known uncertainty.

scenarios.task: Grind a solid into a fine powder before dissolving it.
scenarios.explanation: A mortar and pestle is designed for grinding solids to increase surface area.

scenarios.task: Receive the titrant during a titration (being swirled).
scenarios.explanation: A conical flask can be swirled during titration without splashing.

scenarios.task: Measure approximately 50 cm³ of water for a reaction.
scenarios.explanation: A measuring cylinder gives adequate precision for approximate volumes.

scenarios.task: Add a few drops of indicator to a titration flask.
scenarios.explanation: A dropping pipette allows controlled addition of small amounts of liquid.

scenarios.task: Support a round-bottom flask above a heat source.
scenarios.explanation: A retort stand with clamp holds apparatus securely at the required height.

scenarios.task: Rinse the walls of a conical flask during titration with distilled water.
scenarios.explanation: A wash bottle provides a controlled stream of distilled water for rinsing.

precisionData.note: Burette: 2 readings needed for titre, total ±0.10 cm³
Volumetric pipette: fixed volume, single reading
Graduated pipette: variable volume, moderate precision
Volumetric flask: calibrated at single mark
